sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Low back pain during pregnancy is common and often mechanical, but clinicians should still be alert to non-spinal and obstetric pathology.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Lumbar pain with standing, walking, lifting, or prolonged posture
- Sleep disturbance or reduced tolerance to work tasks
- Stiffness, muscle spasm, or movement fear
- Possible overlap with pelvic girdle pain

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider urinary symptoms, fever, vaginal bleeding, neurological deficit, abdominal pain, or trauma as escalation triggers
- Pregnancy changes biomechanics and load tolerance, but serious pathology is still possible
- Advice should account for gestation, work demands, and safe sleep and transfer positions

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen red flags and whether the patient can still mobilise and work safely
- Encourage posture variation, walking within tolerance, and simple symptom relief strategies
- Escalate for neurological change, trauma, or obstetric red flags

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Differentiate lumbar pain from pelvic girdle pain and referred symptoms
- Use graded exercise, positional advice, movement confidence work, and task modification
- Coordinate medical or obstetric review when symptoms are severe, unusual, or function is rapidly worsening

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Pregnancy back pain management should reduce disability without overmedicalising a common condition
